sentences.text: My name is Abram Jachniuk.
J-A-C-H-N-I-U-K.
Yes.
No.
I was born June 15, 1924.
I was born in Korost, Poland.
I grew up in Korost.
The whole population?
It was probably about-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --close to 1,000 people.
Jewish, were about 15 families.
Most of the people were farmers.
Farmers, some people has the small businesses, like grocery, like that.
We had a farm.
And beside that, my father was a businessman.
He was buying and selling cattle.
In Korost, yes.
Well, in-- in the house we used to speak Yiddish.
But outside, we used to speak Polish.
Polish.
Before the war, everybody was very friendly.
There was no-- no hatred.
It was we never were afraid to go out, to go at nighttime, to go.
We were never-- we didn't-- we weren't afraid.
It just was very friendly.
And it was a good situation.
Yes.
In Korost.
It was a-- we used to go-- [POLISH].
It was a Polish school-- school.
And after this school we used to go in cheder, for a Jewish education.
Not-- not before.
Only the last year was start the antisemitism was bad.
The-- the youth, you know.
They heard about Germany, but it was something-- it was different.
It was not the same, what used to be in the beginning.
I'll tell you, we used to go in cheder.
They used to wait for us, whenever we'll go back.
And they threw stones on us.
And they-- it was already, the last time was so bad we were afraid to go.
Because we were a-- the minority.
And they were-- so it was very hard, you know?
We-- we used to go all other ways, not to, you know, to escape them.
But it was bad already.
This was the-- this was the last year before-- before the Nazis came, with the war.
And
The-- the younger guys, the Ukrainian.
Oh yeah.
All-- you know, all the time, when you are [INAUDIBLE]-- it was not so bad I have to go to the hospital, what.
But they used to hurt us.
I'll tell you another story.
[SIGHS]
One time we-- I think it was the 3rd of May, something.
I remember-- I-- that-- they-- they put the flags, you know, the red flags the communist-- communist flags to-- to show the Polacks that the Jews are communists.
You know?
In the morning, people got up.
Every Jewish home has a red flag.
So you know it.
So the-- the police came right away.
And they let that-- that happen.
The-- the Jews are communists.
But the-- the-- Polish police, they knew it.
And they didn't do anything.
They start to look.
They knew that was made just to-- to make trouble, the Jews.
We were a large family.
It was five sisters and four brothers.
It was very-- you know, it was a large family.
And we were very happy.
You know?
We used to-- it was a happy life.
Well, we used to-- just we-- we-- we were very friendly.
I-- I can not say exactly what, you know, describe things like that.
But--
We keep only kosher.
We keep a kosher home.
We used to all-- always, there.
Our [NON-ENGLISH] used to come always, you know, into you know, to kill the-- to make the [NON-ENGLISH] for the-- for us.
But the-- the last year, that was a time that in-- in Poland, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like, how are there.
The-- the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] is no good, you know?
Because the way they kill it, it's too bad for the-- so they-- they just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we weren't allowed to do anything.
It was a law.
It was a natural law.
But anyway, it was very bad.
But anyway, sure, he used to come nighttime, and we-- we always the kosher-- kosher meat.
Yes.
Shul was in our house.
We had-- we have two Sefer Torahs.
And from-- from the all-- all the Jewish people used to come.
Especially Shabbas, you know, Shabbas, always, Shabbas.
Friday, they came.
It was a--
And our-- our-- mine mother-in-law, she was very-- you know, she was very proud of that.
And my father too.
He was-- you know, we were proud to have the shul in our house.
And later I even don't know what happened to Sefer Torahs, when the Germans came.
They-- we have to-- a long story.
Well, they took us away.
We have to leave everything.
And we couldn't take anything.
And what happened to the Sefer Torahs was just-- just a shame.
My father.
Mine older brother, [?
Josef. ?] He-- he was very educated, he went to Wilno.
He used to read the Torah.
He-- he was very good.
Oh, whenever it was a holiday, you know?
Always we could feel it.
It was a-- a good mood, you know?
It was a-- Yiddish-- Yiddish atmosphere.
Well, for every-- we used to feel the Shabbas.
We used to-- that was a Shabbas meal.
You know, everybody was by the table.
And saying [NON-ENGLISH].
It was like a-- a real Jewish-- the way it's supposed to be.
Our uncle, my uncle, he used to come very often to us.
And he-- he was with us.
He was very often with us.
You know, with-- with his sister.
Because he didn't have a mother, and not a father.
He was by himself.
So he was-- he used to be in our house.
Then he has another sister.
She used to live in [INAUDIBLE], he used to be with her.
But we like her very much.
She used to come here.
He was like a member of the family.
Poland-- Korost was under the Polish government until 1939.
Then when the Russian-- Russian came, you know, Russian invaded Poland.
So they divided Poland.
Half Poland took Russia, and the other half took Germany.
They divided.
This was in 1939.
And then in 1941, the Germans invaded.
And they took over, again.
They-- they took-- they-- they took over Poland.
Life-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Life was-- the-- the Soviet Union was the only people of the world who were with rich.
You know, they have something.
They say you have too much and we have to divide your-- your goods to the poor people.
In the-- in the beginning they start out, because we consider more-- they consider us like we are rich, in that time.
So they start to-- to make like, like a list, one cow for you-- for that.
The other cow for that.
A horse for the other one.
You know, they divided it.
It was a hard time.
But we didn't-- we weren't so-- they-- they shouldn't do that.
Because we didn't belong to that category.
We didn't have--
So my father went.
It was at that time when-- a commissar.
And we went to him.
And he talked to him.
He said, listen, how-- so he gave a-- a document, that we don't belong to that category.
And they shouldn't touch us.
Send-- then was an order to give us back everything, what.
But it was, you know, a lot of things.
So the poor people, when they-- they killed the cow already.
There's nothing to-- so that was-- but this were-- this is--
No.
It was-- in that time, we didn't feel so.
Korost remained under the Soviet Union until-- until the Nazis came, until '41.
With the Germans?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The first thing when the German army came in, you know, we didn't know-- we knew already, it's not-- but we came out We want to see them.
Right away they-- they start-- I don't know, they recognized us.
Jude, Jude.
And they hey, Jude, come.
And-- and they went to our house.
They went to our house.
They want that.
They want that.
They want schnapps.
There was everything.
Whatever they ask, we gave them.
And they start to scare us, with [INAUDIBLE].
We're going to kill you.
We're going to-- Well, they-- you know, they left us-- they left us alone.
They didn't do anything to us.
So but we knew already, that we are in trouble.
We were afraid.
We-- we didn't go out anymore to-- to see them.
We were afraid.
We didn't-- we were afraid to sleep in our house.
So the-- the first night, I and my brother-- my brother and I, we went to a Christian, you know, what we were in good times.
And we ask him if we can-- we can sleep.
So he said, if you want to sleep, you can go you know, in the barn, with the cows, and with the horses.
And the-- there is hay.
You can sleep in the hay.
So we went-- and they-- everybody, you know, went somewhere else, from our family.
In the morning-- so we slept all night.
In the morning we-- we heard Germans.
A lot of Germans are talking.
And they-- they are around the house.
So we were already, alert.
We were afraid already.
So later we heard the Germans are going on the ladder.
Have to go to us, you know?
So because they were with the horses, and they want hay for the horses.
They didn't think about us.
But that-- so but we were already-- they will come, and they're going to kill us with the-- so what-- what-- what can you do?
But the-- the roof was, in that time, and it was from straw.
You know, the roof was made from straw.
So we-- we made a-- a hole.
And we jumped.
And brother and I, we jumped.
There was a guard.
The guard, they notice.
They notice us and they started to go hard, hard, hard.
And they start to shoot over at us.
We were running, and they after us.
There was a-- a little-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
How do you call?
We had to escape.
A-- a-- a fence.
So I-- I jump over the fence.
And I-- and my brother doesn't-- and I didn't see my brother.
They got my brother.
So I-- I run away.
And I was all-- this was in the morning.
All day long, I was in the-- in the fields, you know, corn fields. you know, corn fields was that.
So I was hiding.
And I didn't know what to do.
What happened to my brother?
You know, I was afraid to go-- you were-- you were afraid to go.
Doesn't matter where, but in that time.
So I were all day long.
In the evening, it got a little bit darker.
I came in. l look, you know, I didn't know who is whom.
I saw the brother is home, the brother in the family.
So I ask, they caught him.
They caught him and they beat him up.
And they-- they need for the horses, hay.
They need things.
So-- so they took him to the richer people, that were-- who-- to tell them, to give them-- to give them hay.
I don't know, whatever they need for the horses.
He went.
He-- he told this story to the people.
He knew what, he used to deal with them, you know, are in good terms.
And they saw that he's in trouble.
You know?
They gave them what-- and they want to kill him.
But one German said, that you are lucky I am here.
We let you go.
And they-- they beat him up, but they didn't kill them that time.
How old were you then?
How old?
I was that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --about 15, 16 years at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So since then was-- we were afraid.
It was terrible.
Yeah, another thing, then one-- one night-- we-- we went together to-- to our Jewish family.
You know?
We were already a few families, together.
So another Jewish--
We didn't sleep because we were-- we knew that something going to happen, and-- and we didn't sleep already.
And we heard the planes, you know, are-- planes are coming.
So OK, we heard planes are coming in the morning.
We heard that the-- they send-- the Russians send paratroopers near Korost there, by in the woods.
And the Ukrainian noticed that.
And they organized-- all, everybody, especially the police, what they used to work, they organized to-- to-- to kill these, the-- the paratroopers.
Perhaps somebody saw them and they caught them.
But one paratrooper, I remember, he shoot one of the leaders of the Ukrainian.
I know.
I remember who the leader was.
Eh-- [UKRAINIAN], they used to call him [UKRAINIAN].
I-- so-- they called him-- they-- they shoot that leader.
No, they were very-- so I don't remember how many they kill, how many of the paratroopers they kill.
And they went to look for Jewish people to-- to bury them, to bury the paratroopers.
Oh, they-- they took-- they took mine brother and another to bury.
And they-- they were afraid they're going to kill them too.
But they didn't kill that time, you know?
They make all kind of fun of them.
But didn't kill them.
Next day, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a group of the Ukrainian, the youth, organized.
They took-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] over a-- a truck.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And they-- they went to look for whoever they can catch.
You know?
Mhm.
Like a-- are tender, you know?
They-- and whoever they-- and they-- they caught three-- three Jews.
[INAUDIBLE], from [POLISH].
This is the next village.
And it was a label, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was our next-door neighbor.
They stop in our house.
They couldn't-- they couldn't-- nobody was out.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
You know, one that was together with them, he came, he told us.
You know, it was a time that we were friends.
You know, when the Germans came, everything-- you know, they-- they-- we became their enemies.
So and they shoot the-- they-- they shoot them.
Just like that, they told them to run.
And everybody try who can-- who can do better, a better job.
This was later.
So we weren't, anymore, in the houses.
You know?
We were hiding where we can.
But later, wherever they can catch us to-- to-- they took us to-- to clean the streets.
You know, there was one street.
But it was dirty.
You know, in a village, how it is.
So the-- the Jews has to do the-- they dirty work-- work.
Huh?
Oh, it was a order from the-- from the police, you know.
From the Ukrainian.
And one of them used to-- he used to be a friend of mine, sitting in-- I mean, he was watching us, you know, if we do a good job.
They were-- they were the Ukrainian-- they were-- they promised them that they're going to have Ukrainians, you know?
And they-- if they're going to help them out, they're going-- they're going to have a Ukrainian.
So they did what-- what they told them to do.
